content.text: When you first encounter Azure, you will notice the sign-in page accepts two very different kinds of credentials: a personal Microsoft Account (MSA) and a Work or School Account backed by Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory). Understanding the difference is not just administrative housekeeping — it determines which Azure features you can use, how authentication is enforced, and who controls your identity. This sub-subtopic explains both account types in depth and shows exactly how Azure treats each one.
Account Types at a Glance

attrs.alt: Comparison diagram: Microsoft Account (blue, left) vs Entra ID Work/School Account (purple, right)

content.text: The diagram above summarises the six most important properties of each account type.
Blue
= Microsoft Account (personal consumer identity).
Purple
= Entra ID Work or School Account (organisational identity).
What is a Microsoft Account (MSA)?

A Microsoft Account is a free, personally owned identity. You create it at account.microsoft.com and associate it with any email address — an @outlook.com, @hotmail.com, @live.com address that Microsoft provisions for you, or an external address such as user@gmail.com that you verify and enrol as an MSA. The account is yours permanently; no employer or school can revoke it.
Primary consumer services: Xbox, OneDrive Personal, Skype, Microsoft 365 Personal and Family subscriptions.
Authentication is managed by the individual — you set your own password, recovery email, and optionally a TOTP authenticator app.
No Conditional Access policies can be applied by an external admin because there is no admin.
The account lives in the Microsoft consumer identity system, not in any Entra ID tenant.
MSAs are sometimes called 'live IDs' in older Microsoft documentation and APIs.
You can sign into the Azure Portal with an MSA, but your use of enterprise Azure features is limited (see the feature table below).
Inspecting your account type with Azure CLI
Run the commands below immediately after signing in to determine which account type is active in the current shell session.

# Sign in (browser opens)
az login

# Show the active subscription and the identity that owns it
az account show --output table

# The 'user.type' field distinguishes MSA from work/school:
#   user  -> work or school account (Entra ID member)
#   msa   -> personal Microsoft Account
az account show --query '{name:name, userType:user.type, tenantId:tenantId}' --output json
If
userType
comes back as
"msa"
, you are signed in with a personal Microsoft Account. If it says
"user"
, you are using a work or school account managed by an Entra ID tenant.
What is a Work or School Account (Entra ID Identity)?

A Work or School Account is an identity that an organisation's Entra ID tenant owns. An IT administrator creates it, and the user logs in with a corporate or school domain such as alice@contoso.com. Unlike an MSA, the organisation controls every aspect of this identity — password policies, MFA enforcement, Conditional Access, account lifetime, and which applications the account can access.
Lives inside a named Entra ID tenant (a directory identified by a GUID and an initial domain like contoso.onmicrosoft.com).
Can be a 'Member' (created inside the tenant) or a 'Guest' (invited from outside — see B2B section).
Subject to admin-configured Conditional Access policies: location restrictions, device compliance, sign-in risk checks.
Eligible for Entra ID Premium features including Privileged Identity Management (PIM), Identity Protection, and access reviews.
Sign-in URL can be scoped to a specific tenant: https://portal.azure.com/<tenantId> forces authentication against the correct directory.
Microsoft 365 Enterprise licenses (E3, E5) are assigned to work accounts, not MSAs.
Administrators can disable or delete the account instantly if an employee leaves.
MSA vs Work or School Account — Feature Comparison
Dimension
Microsoft Account (MSA)
Work / School Account (Entra ID)
Purpose
Consumer services, personal productivity
Corporate / institutional access
Managed by
Individual user
IT administrator via Entra ID
Domain
@outlook.com, @hotmail.com, or any enrolled email
@company.com or @tenant.onmicrosoft.com
MFA enforcement
User-chosen (optional)
Admin-enforced via Authentication Policies
Conditional Access
Not supported
Fully supported (location, device, risk)
Privileged Identity Management
Not available
Available (Entra ID P2 required)
Entra ID Premium features
Not available
Available with P1/P2 license
Azure RBAC assignment
Limited — cannot be assigned to some resource scopes
Full RBAC support across all resource scopes
Sign-in URL
https://portal.azure.com (defaults to MSA context)
https://portal.azure.com/<tenantId> for org context
Account lifetime
User-owned, permanent until user deletes it
Admin-controlled, expires on offboarding
B2B guest support
Can be invited as guest into an Entra ID tenant
Can be invited as guest into another tenant
Microsoft 365 Enterprise
Not eligible for E3/E5 licenses
Directly licensed from Entra ID
How Azure Uses Each Account Type

Azure subscriptions are always anchored to an Entra ID tenant. When you create an Azure account with a personal MSA, Microsoft silently provisions a hidden Entra ID tenant just for you. This lets Azure's RBAC and billing systems work consistently regardless of the identity type used at sign-up. However, that auto-created tenant is limited: it has no Entra ID Premium license, no organisational policies, and cannot enrol other members as employees.

Caveat: If you start an Azure trial with your personal MSA and later want to use enterprise features (PIM, Conditional Access, B2B), you will need to create or associate a proper Entra ID tenant with a work account. Migrating a subscription between tenants is possible but disruptive — plan the account type before going to production.
MSA sign-up: Azure creates a shadow tenant (*.onmicrosoft.com) owned by the MSA — adequate for personal labs, not for teams.
Work account sign-up: Subscription is associated with the organisation's existing Entra ID tenant from day one.
Mixed teams: You can invite MSA users as B2B guests into an organisation's tenant — they get constrained access under that tenant's policies.
Service Principals: These are non-human identities always created inside a specific Entra ID tenant, never tied to an MSA.
Managed Identities: Same — always tenant-scoped; the MSA concept is irrelevant for workload identities.
CLI: Identifying Account Type Programmatically
az account show — tenant and user type

# Full JSON output — look at user.type
az account show

# Targeted query
az account show --query '{subscription:name, tenantId:tenantId, userType:user.type, userName:user.name}'
az ad user show — member vs guest

# Works for work/school accounts only (MSA does not have an Entra ID object unless it is a guest)
az ad user show --id alice@contoso.com --query '{displayName:displayName, userType:userType, userPrincipalName:userPrincipalName}'

# userType will be:
#   "Member"  -> full organisational account created in this tenant
#   "Guest"   -> external identity (could be another org's work account or an MSA) invited via B2B
Microsoft Graph API — distinguishing MSA from Entra ID member

# Acquire a token (requires Entra ID work account with User.Read.All)
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://graph.microsoft.com --query accessToken -o tsv)

# Fetch user object — 'identities' array shows the issuer
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/alice@contoso.com?\$select=displayName,userType,identities"   | python3 -m json.tool

# MSA guest will show issuer "MicrosoftAccount" in the identities array
# Member account will show issuer matching the tenant's verified domain
Signing Into the Azure Portal: MSA vs Work Account
The Azure Portal (portal.azure.com) accepts both account types, but the experience diverges immediately after the credential prompt.
MSA sign-in: Portal defaults to the shadow Entra ID tenant auto-created for that MSA. The directory switcher in the top-right corner shows only that tenant.
Work account sign-in: Portal defaults to the tenant the account belongs to. If the account is a guest in multiple tenants, the directory switcher lists all of them.
Scoped sign-in (work accounts): Navigating to https://portal.azure.com/<tenantId> skips the tenant-picker and lands directly in the right directory.
Scoped sign-in for MSA is not meaningful — the MSA tenant GUID changes per account and provides no enterprise functionality.

Multi-factor authentication prompt: For MSA, the MFA challenge is whatever the user configured. For work accounts, the MFA method and frequency are dictated by the admin's Authentication Strength policy.
Classic Azure (portal.azure.com) vs Entra ID portal (entra.microsoft.com): The Entra ID portal requires a work account; MSA access is blocked for most Entra admin blades.
Choosing the Right Account: MSA vs Work Account
The choice is usually constrained by context, not preference.
Personal learning / free trial: MSA is fine. Spin up VMs, experiment with services, and stay within the free tier.
Team or business production workload: Always use a work account in a properly configured Entra ID tenant, even if the 'team' is just two founders.
Enterprise procurement: Azure subscriptions bought through an Enterprise Agreement (EA) or CSP must be associated with an Entra ID tenant — MSA cannot be the billing account for EA.
Azure DevOps (dev.azure.com): Accepts both, but advanced features (Conditional Access, Entra-linked groups) require work accounts.

Cannot convert an MSA to a work account: The two identity systems are separate. You can create a new work account and transfer Azure resources (move subscriptions between tenants) but the MSA itself remains a consumer identity.
Rule of thumb: if anyone other than you will ever touch the subscription, or if the subscription will run production workloads, start with a work account and an Entra ID tenant from day one.
Azure Features: MSA vs Work Account Availability
Feature
MSA
Work / School Account
Azure RBAC (Owner, Contributor, Reader)
Yes (on personal subscription only)
Yes (across any subscription the admin grants)
Entra ID Premium P1 (Conditional Access)
No
Yes (requires P1 license on tenant)
Entra ID Premium P2 (PIM, Identity Protection)
No
Yes (requires P2 license on tenant)
Privileged Identity Management (PIM)
No
Yes
B2B Collaboration (invite external users)
No (cannot be the inviter from an MSA-only tenant)
Yes
B2C (customer-facing apps)
MSA can authenticate as a B2C consumer
Admin configures B2C tenant with work account
Enterprise Application SSO (SAML/OIDC)
No
Yes
Microsoft Defender for Cloud
View only on personal sub
Full integration with Entra ID policies
Azure Policy (org-wide governance)
Not meaningful on MSA tenant
Yes — applied across management groups
Microsoft Sentinel
Can deploy to personal sub
Full RBAC and Entra integration
Azure AD (Entra ID) Connect (hybrid identity)
Not applicable
Yes — syncs on-prem AD to Entra ID
Microsoft Intune (device management)
No
Yes
Best Practices for Organisations

Never use personal Microsoft Accounts for production Azure workloads. An MSA is tied to an individual; if that person leaves or loses access to their personal email, the subscription can become permanently inaccessible. Always assign ownership to a work account managed by your organisation's Entra ID tenant.
Create a dedicated Entra ID tenant (or use your existing Microsoft 365 tenant) before creating the first Azure subscription.
Assign at least two Global Administrators using work accounts — single-admin tenants are a business continuity risk.
Enable Entra ID P1 at minimum for all admin accounts to unlock Conditional Access and protect the control plane.
Use Privileged Identity Management (PIM) with Entra ID P2 for just-in-time privileged role activation instead of standing Owner assignments.
Audit guest (B2B) accounts quarterly via Entra ID Access Reviews to remove stale MSA guests.
Block MSA sign-in to the Azure Portal using a Conditional Access policy scoped to the Azure Management application ID (797f4846-ba00-4fd7-ba43-dac1f8f63013).
CLI: Creating a Work Account and Assigning It Azure Roles

# Sign in as a Global Administrator (work account required)
az login --tenant your-tenant-id

# Create a new user in the Entra ID tenant
az ad user create   --display-name "Alice Engineer"   --user-principal-name alice@contoso.com   --password "ChangeMe123!"   --force-change-password-next-sign-in true

# Retrieve the new user's object ID
OBJECT_ID=$(az ad user show --id alice@contoso.com --query id -o tsv)

# Get the subscription ID
SUBSCRIPTION_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

# Assign the Contributor role at subscription scope
az role assignment create   --assignee "$OBJECT_ID"   --role Contributor   --scope "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION_ID"

# Verify the assignment
az role assignment list --assignee alice@contoso.com --output table

The user alice@contoso.com is now a work account member of the Entra ID tenant and has Contributor access to the subscription. She can sign into portal.azure.com with that credential and will be subject to any Conditional Access policies the admin configures.
Guest Users (B2B): MSA Users in an Entra ID Tenant

Microsoft Entra B2B collaboration lets you invite external identities — including personal MSA users — into your Entra ID tenant as guests. Once invited and accepted, the guest appears in your tenant's user directory with userType = 'Guest'. You can assign them Azure RBAC roles, add them to security groups, and apply Conditional Access policies scoped to guest accounts.
The MSA user authenticates against Microsoft's consumer identity system; your tenant does not manage their password or MFA.
You control what the guest can do inside your tenant (RBAC, group membership, app assignments) but not how they authenticate on their end.
Guest access can be restricted using Entra ID's External Collaboration Settings and Conditional Access Guest/External User conditions.
Guests see your tenant in their portal.azure.com directory switcher alongside their own MSA tenant.
B2B guests do not consume a full Entra ID user license — billing is based on Monthly Active Users for premium features.
CLI: Inviting an MSA User as a B2B Guest

# Invite an MSA user as a guest (signed in as admin of the target tenant)
az rest --method POST   --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/invitations"   --headers "Content-Type=application/json"   --body '{
    "invitedUserDisplayName": "Bob Personal",
    "invitedUserEmailAddress": "bob@outlook.com",
    "inviteRedirectUrl": "https://portal.azure.com",
    "sendInvitationMessage": true
  }'

# After Bob accepts, retrieve his guest object
az ad user show --id bob_outlook.com#EXT#@contoso.onmicrosoft.com   --query '{displayName:displayName, userType:userType, userPrincipalName:userPrincipalName}'

# Assign him Reader on a specific resource group
az role assignment create   --assignee bob_outlook.com#EXT#@contoso.onmicrosoft.com   --role Reader   --scope "/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION_ID/resourceGroups/my-rg"
The guest UPN follows the pattern
externalEmail#EXT#@yourTenant.onmicrosoft.com
. Use this UPN (or the guest's object ID) in all subsequent
az role assignment
and
az ad
commands.
Entra ID Tenant: The Container Behind Work Accounts

Every Work or School Account lives inside an Entra ID tenant. A tenant is a dedicated instance of Microsoft Entra ID that an organisation receives when it signs up for any Microsoft cloud service. It is identified by a globally unique GUID (the tenant ID) and at least one verified domain (e.g., contoso.com). Understanding the tenant is essential because it is the trust boundary: RBAC assignments, Conditional Access policies, PIM configurations, and app registrations are all scoped to a tenant.
Every Azure subscription trusts exactly one Entra ID tenant for authentication — you cannot attach a subscription to two tenants simultaneously.
A single tenant can hold multiple subscriptions: all share the same directory of users, groups, and service principals.
The tenant's initial domain is always <tenant-name>.onmicrosoft.com; you can add verified custom domains (e.g., contoso.com).
MSA users do not belong to your tenant natively — they appear only if invited as B2B guests.
Tenant ID is visible in the Azure Portal under Azure Active Directory > Overview, or via 'az account show'.

# Retrieve your tenant ID from the CLI
az account show --query tenantId -o tsv

# List all tenants your current login can access
az account list --query '[].{name:name, tenantId:tenantId}' --output table

# Show tenant details via Microsoft Graph
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://graph.microsoft.com --query accessToken -o tsv)
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/organization?$select=displayName,verifiedDomains,tenantType"   | python3 -m json.tool
Common Mistakes When Mixing MSA and Work Accounts
Starting a production subscription with an MSA and then trying to add Conditional Access — the feature simply does not exist on the MSA-owned shadow tenant.
Assigning Azure roles to an MSA directly (e.g., owner@gmail.com) instead of inviting it as a B2B guest first — the assignment silently fails or creates an orphaned entry.
Signing into the wrong directory in the Azure Portal and wondering why resources are missing — always check the directory name in the top-right corner.
Assuming the MSA password reset affects the Azure subscription — MSA password resets go through account.microsoft.com, not Azure AD Self-Service Password Reset.
Using an MSA for Azure DevOps pipelines expecting Entra ID Managed Identity integration — service connections backed by MSA cannot use Managed Identity or Workload Identity Federation.
Expecting Entra ID logs (Sign-in logs, Audit logs) to capture MSA authentication events — MSA authentications against the consumer identity system are not surfaced in Entra ID diagnostic logs.
Managing Multiple Accounts in the Azure CLI

Developers often have both a personal MSA and one or more work accounts. The Azure CLI supports multiple concurrent sessions — each tenant you have signed into appears as a separate subscription entry.

# Sign in with a second account (e.g., your work account) without losing the MSA session
az login --tenant contoso.com

# List all cached accounts/subscriptions across all tenants
az account list --all --output table

# Switch the active subscription to your work account's subscription
az account set --subscription "Contoso Production"

# Verify active context
az account show --query '{sub:name, tenant:tenantId, user:user.name}' -o json

# Sign out of a specific account
az logout --username alice@contoso.com

Each call to 'az login' adds credentials to the local token cache (~/.azure/). The active subscription determines which context CLI commands run against — always confirm the context before running destructive commands like 'az group delete'.
Authentication Flows: MSA vs Work Account

The sign-in experience and token acquisition differ between Microsoft Accounts and Entra ID work accounts. Applications that need to support both must use the Microsoft Authentication Library (MSAL) with the common or organizations authority endpoints.

# --- Inspect the token issued to the current az login session ---
# The 'tid' claim identifies tenant; MSA logins use the MSA tenant ID
az account get-access-token --query '{Tenant:tenant,ExpiresOn:expiresOn}'

# Decode the JWT to see claims (requires jq and base64)
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
echo $TOKEN | cut -d'.' -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"tid"|"oid"|"upn"|"idp"|"ver"'

# Key claims:
# tid: tenant ID — for MSA personal tokens this is '9188040d-6c67-4c5b-b112-36a304b66dad' (MSA tenant)
# idp: identity provider — MSA tokens include 'https://sts.windows.net/9188040d...'
# upn / unique_name: for MSA may be a @outlook.com or @live.com address
# ver: token version; v2.0 tokens use 'sub' not 'oid' for MSA users

# Check if the signed-in account is an MSA or work account
az account show --query 'user.{Name:name,Type:type}'# Type 'user' = interactive login; Type 'servicePrincipal' = SP/managed identity

# Python: MSAL — acquire token supporting both MSA and Entra ID
# pip install msal
import msal

# 'common' authority accepts both personal MSA and work/school accounts
# Use 'organizations' to restrict to work/school only
# Use 'consumers' to restrict to personal MSA only
app = msal.PublicClientApplication(
    client_id='<your-app-client-id>',
    authority='https://login.microsoftonline.com/common',  # or /organizations or /consumers
)

# Interactive browser login
result = app.acquire_token_interactive(scopes=['User.Read'])

if 'access_token' in result:
    import base64, json
    # Decode JWT payload (no signature verification — for inspection only)
    payload = result['access_token'].split('.')[1]
    # Add padding if needed
    payload += '=' * (4 - len(payload) % 4)
    claims = json.loads(base64.b64decode(payload))
    print('Tenant ID:', claims.get('tid'))
    print('Identity provider:', claims.get('idp', 'Entra ID (no idp claim = work account)'))
    print('Account type:', 'MSA' if claims.get('idp') else 'Work/School (Entra ID)')
else:
    print('Error:', result.get('error_description'))
Multi-Tenant Applications and Account Type Restrictions
Azure App Registrations support four audience configurations that control which account types can sign in. Choosing incorrectly is a common source of authentication failures.
Audience Setting
Supported Account Types
Authority Endpoint
Use Case
Single tenant
Work/school in THIS tenant only
https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>
Internal LOB apps, no external users
Multitenant (Entra ID)
Work/school in ANY Entra ID tenant
https://login.microsoftonline.com/organizations
SaaS apps sold to enterprises
Multitenant + MSA
Work/school in any tenant + personal MSA
https://login.microsoftonline.com/common
Consumer-facing apps (e.g., OneDrive SDK)
MSA only
Personal Microsoft Accounts only
https://login.microsoftonline.com/consumers
Consumer apps with no org users

# Create an app registration: single tenant (work accounts in this tenant only)
az ad app create \
  --display-name 'internal-portal' \
  --sign-in-audience AzureADMyOrg

# Create an app registration: multitenant (any Entra ID tenant)
az ad app create \
  --display-name 'saas-product' \
  --sign-in-audience AzureADMultipleOrgs

# Create an app: accept both MSA and work accounts
az ad app create \
  --display-name 'consumer-and-enterprise' \
  --sign-in-audience AzureADandPersonalMicrosoftAccount

# Update an existing app's audience
az ad app update \
  --id <app-id> \
  --sign-in-audience AzureADMultipleOrgs

# Check an app's current audience setting
az ad app show \
  --id <app-id> \
  --query '{Audience:signInAudience, AppId:appId, Name:displayName}'

If your app registration uses AzureADMyOrg (single tenant) and a user tries to sign in with an @outlook.com personal account, they receive AADSTS50020: 'User account does not exist in tenant'. Change the audience to AzureADandPersonalMicrosoftAccount if personal accounts must be supported.
Switching Between Accounts in the Azure CLI

When working with multiple identities — a personal MSA for testing and a work account for production — the Azure CLI supports multiple cached sessions. You can switch between them without re-authenticating each time.

# Log in with a work account
az login --tenant <work-tenant-id>

# Log in with a personal MSA (opens browser to personal login)
az login --allow-no-subscriptions

# List all accounts the CLI has cached (all logged-in identities)
az account list --all -o table

# Switch active account to a specific subscription/identity
az account set --subscription <work-sub-id>

# Confirm which account is active
az account show --query 'user.name' -o tsv

# Log out a specific account
az logout --username user@company.com

# Log out all cached accounts
az logout

# Use a specific tenant for a single command without switching context
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me' \
  --resource https://graph.microsoft.com \
  --tenant <specific-tenant-id>
Entra ID External Identities: Bringing MSA into the Org

Organizations that need to collaborate with users who only have personal Microsoft Accounts can use Entra ID External Identities (B2B). MSA users are invited as guest users into the organization's tenant, where standard RBAC and Conditional Access can be applied to them.

# Invite a personal MSA user as a B2B guest
az ad invitation create \
  --invited-user-email-address consumer@outlook.com \
  --invite-redirect-url 'https://portal.azure.com' \
  --invited-user-display-name 'External Contractor'

# After acceptance, list guest users in the tenant
az ad user list \
  --filter "userType eq 'Guest'" \
  --query '[].{Name:displayName,Email:mail,UPN:userPrincipalName}' \
  -o table

# Assign a resource role to the guest (Reader on a resource group)
GUEST_OID=$(az ad user show \
  --id consumer@outlook.com \
  --query id -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role Reader \
  --assignee $GUEST_OID \
  --resource-group rg-shared-project

# Configure B2B settings: restrict which domains can be invited
az rest --method PUT \
  --url 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/crossTenantAccessPolicy/default' \
  --body '{"b2bCollaborationOutbound":{"usersAndGroups":{"accessType":"allowed","targets":[{"target":"AllUsers","targetType":"user"}]}}}'

# Remove a guest user when collaboration ends
az ad user delete --id $GUEST_OID
Guest User Property
MSA Guest
Entra ID Guest (from another org)
UPN format in home tenant
user_outlook.com#EXT#@yourorg.onmicrosoft.com
user_partnerorg.com#EXT#@yourorg.onmicrosoft.com
userType claim
Guest
Guest
Identity managed by
Microsoft (MSA), uncontrolled by host
Partner's Entra ID tenant
MFA enforcement
Host tenant Conditional Access applies
Host tenant Conditional Access applies
Password reset
User resets via account.microsoft.com
Partner IT manages it
PIM eligible assignments
Yes, with restrictions
Yes
Licensing required
Entra ID P1/P2 for advanced features
Entra ID P1/P2 for advanced features
Summary

A Microsoft Account is a personal consumer identity you own and manage yourself; it gives you access to Xbox, OneDrive Personal, and Microsoft 365 Personal. A Work or School Account is an organisational identity managed by an IT administrator inside a Microsoft Entra ID tenant; it unlocks the full Azure enterprise feature set including Conditional Access, Privileged Identity Management, and organisation-wide RBAC governance. Azure accepts both at sign-in, but the account type you choose at subscription creation has long-term consequences: personal subscriptions under an MSA cannot use enterprise governance features, and migrating between tenants later is disruptive. For any professional or team workload, always start with a work account in a properly configured Entra ID tenant.
